title: Excess mortality: Deaths from all causes compared to average over previous years, by age
subtitle: The percentage difference between the reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 — broken down by age group — and the average number of deaths in the same period over the years 2015–2019.
note: The reported number of deaths might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting.
caption:
Excess mortality: Deaths from all causes compared to average over previous years, by age

The percentage difference between the reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 — broken down by age group — and the average number of deaths in the same period over the years 2015–2019.

This chart presents six line graphs tracking excess mortality for the United Kingdom (orange) and Germany (blue) from early 2020 through late 2023, broken down by age group. The vertical axis measures the percentage difference from the 2015–2019 average, ranging from -50% to 150%. In the "Ages 0–14" panel, both lines fluctuate closely around the 0% baseline with minor peaks. The "Ages 15–64" panel shows a distinct spike for the United Kingdom in early 2020 reaching approximately 75%, while Germany remains lower. For "Ages 65–74," the United Kingdom line peaks near 100% in early 2020, followed by a period where Germany exhibits higher peaks in 2022 and 2023. The "Ages 75–84" and "Ages 85+" panels display the most extreme values; in early 2020, the United Kingdom line surges past 100% in both categories, whereas Germany shows a more gradual rise with peaks occurring later in the timeline, particularly in 2022 for the 85+ group. The "All ages" panel aggregates these trends, showing the initial 2020 peak for the United Kingdom exceeding 100%, followed by subsequent waves where the blue line for Germany frequently sits above the orange line. Throughout the timeline, the lines cross multiple times, indicating shifts in which country experienced higher relative mortality in specific weeks. The reported number of deaths might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting.
